1: bĕrît kāraṯî lĕʿênāy — 'I cut a covenant with my eyes' — kārat bĕrît is the standard idiom for formal covenant-making (Gen 15:18; 21:27; Exod 24:8); applied to the eyes it elevates visual discipline to the status of a binding legal agreement. The eyes as parties to a covenant is uniquely rigorous: not 'I tried not to look' but 'I am covenantally obligated not to look.' ûmāh ʾetbônān ʿal-bĕṯûlāh — 'how then could I gaze at a young woman?' — bĕṯûlāh (a young woman of marriageable age, often translated 'virgin'); bîn in the hitpo'el = to look at intently/contemplate. The eye-covenant becomes the foundation for Jesus's intensification of the prohibition in Matt 5:28 ('everyone who looks at a woman to lust after her has already committed adultery with her').
2: wĕmāh ḥēleq ʾĕlôah mimāʿal — 'what is God's portion from above?' — ḥēleq (allotment/portion, the term used for Levitical inheritance and land distribution); God's 'portion' here means what God assigns as consequence. wĕnaḥălat Šaddai mimmĕrōmîm — 'and the inheritance of Shaddai from the heights?' — naḥălāh (inheritance/heritage, the covenantal land-promise word); the verse grounds Job's ethical behavior not in personal virtue alone but in the framework of divine reward-structure (even as he maintains his integrity despite that structure apparently having failed him).
3: hălō-ʾêḏ lĕʿawwāl — 'is not ruin for the wrongdoer?' — ʾêḏ (calamity/disaster, the destruction that arrives); ʿawwāl (one who is crooked/unjust; from ʿāwal = to be bent, the root of ʿawlāh = injustice). wĕnēḵar lĕp̄ōʿălê ʾāwen — 'and disaster for those who work iniquity?' — nēḵar (foreign/strange, used as a noun for 'estrangement/alienness'); pōʿălê ʾāwen (workers of trouble/iniquity, a fixed phrase from the Psalms: 5:5; 6:8; 14:4). Job is affirming the retributive framework as a general principle — but his experience contradicts its application to his specific case.
4: hălō-hûʾ yirʾeh ḏĕrāḵay — 'does he not see my ways?' — the rhetorical certainty that God observes: this is simultaneously the ground of Job's confidence in his innocence (God sees that I am not guilty) and his perplexity (God sees but does not respond appropriately). wĕḵol-ṣĕʿādāy yispôr — 'and counts all my steps?' — sāpar (to count/number) of the divine accounting of human movement; the same forensic oversight Job complained about in 14:16 is here offered as a reason for confidence in his vindication — since God's scrutiny would reveal nothing amiss.
5: ʾim-hālaktî ʿim-šāwʾ — 'if I have walked with falsehood/emptiness' — šāwʾ (emptiness/vanity/falsehood; used in the third commandment 'do not take the name of the LORD in vain/šāwʾ'; and in Job 7:3; 15:31; 35:13); applying it to walking = living by deception. wattĕḥaš ʿal-mirmāh raglî — 'and my foot has rushed toward deceit' — mirmāh (deceit/treachery; the same word used of Jacob's deception in Gen 27:35 — 'your brother came with deceit/mirmāh'); the ethical autobiography continues with a negative conditional catalogue.
6: yišqĕlēnî bĕmōʾznê ṣeḏeq — 'let me be weighed in honest/just scales' — mōʾznê ṣeḏeq (scales of righteousness/justice) is the idiom of Lev 19:36 and Prov 16:11 ('honest scales and balances are the LORD's'); Job invites the divine weighing that Prov 21:2 warns about. wĕyēḏaʿ ʾĕlôah tummāṯî — 'and let God know my integrity' — tummāh (integrity/wholeness, from tāmam = to be complete; the same root as Job's description in 1:1 and 1:8); the invitation to divine knowledge of his integrity is both a legal demand and an act of radical transparency.
7: ʾim tāṭeh ʾaššurî min-haddāreḵ — 'if my step has turned from the path' — ʾaššûr (step/walk, poetic synonym for going; cf. Ps 17:5 'my steps have held to your paths'); min-haddāreḵ (from the right path). wĕʾaḥar ʿênay hālaḵ libbî — 'and my heart has followed my eyes' — the inversion of proper order: the heart should govern the eyes (not vice versa); when the eyes lead the heart, desire precedes judgment. ûḇĕḵappay dāḇaq mûm — 'and any stain has clung to my hands' — mûm (blemish/defect, the word for a physical blemish disqualifying a priest in Lev 21:17-23; applied here morally).
8: ʾezrāʿāh wĕʾaḥēr yōʾḵēl — 'let me sow and another eat' — the curse-formula for violated covenantal labor (Deut 28:30,33,51: the covenant curses include others consuming what you plant); Job invites these curses upon himself if guilty. wĕṣeʾĕṣāʾay yĕšōrāšû — 'and let my offspring be uprooted' — ṣeʾĕṣāʾ (offspring/descendants, from the root of 'going out'); šāraš in the po'al = to be uprooted by the roots; dynastic obliteration as the sworn consequence.
9: ʾim-nip̄tāh libbî ʿal-ʾiššāh — 'if my heart has been enticed by a woman' — pāṯāh (to entice/seduce; the same verb used in Exod 22:16 for seducing a virgin, and in Prov 1:10 for sinners enticing the young); the heart as the primary site of sexual temptation, not just the eye (v1). wĕʾarāḇtî ʿal-pétaḥ rēʿî — 'or if I have lurked at my neighbor's door' — ʾāraḇ (to lie in wait/ambush, the verb of setting a trap; used in Prov 1:11,18; 7:12 for the wayward woman); lurking at the neighbor's door = the precise act prohibited in the commandment against coveting the neighbor's wife.
10: tiṭḥan lĕʾaḥēr ʾišṯî — 'let my wife grind for another' — ṭāḥan (to grind grain, the work of a slave-concubine in Exod 11:5; Num 11:8; Isa 47:2); to grind for another = to be sexually enslaved to another. wĕʿālêhā yiḵrĕʿûn ʾăḥērîm — 'and let others bow over her' — kāraʿ (to bow down/kneel; used in sexual contexts in Gen 49:9; Judg 5:27); the sworn curse-consequence of adultery is his own wife's sexual degradation. The brutal reciprocity of the oath formula reflects how seriously the social fabric of family and neighbor honor was taken.
11: kî-hîʾ zimmāh — 'for that would be a deliberate outrage' — zimmāh is the strongest OT word for sexual wickedness: deliberate, calculated, outrageous depravity (Lev 18:17; 19:29; 20:14; Ezek 16:27; 22:9; Prov 10:23); distinguished from mere passion by its premeditated character. wĕhîʾ ʿāwōn pĕlîlîm — 'and an iniquity deserving judges' — pĕlîlîm (judges/referees; from pālal = to judge/mediate); the phrase 'iniquity of/for judges' = a crime serious enough to require judicial adjudication, not merely personal repentance.
12: kî ʾēš hîʾ ʾaḏ-ʾăḇaddôn tōʾḵēl — 'for it is a fire that burns all the way to Abaddon' — ʾăḇaddôn (destruction/the underworld realm, from ʾāḇaḏ = to perish; used in Job 26:6; 28:22; Ps 88:11; Prov 15:11; Rev 9:11 where it names the angel of the bottomless pit as Abaddon). The fire of sexual betrayal burns to the depths of the realm of destruction — its consequences are eschatologically profound. wĕḇĕḵol-tĕḇûʾāṯî tĕšarrēš — 'and would consume all my harvest' — šāraš in the pi'el = to uproot entirely; tĕḇûʾāh = produce/income/harvest. Total economic destruction as the consequence of sexual sin.
13: ʾim-ʾemʾas mišpaṭ ʿaḇdî wĕʾāmāṯî — 'if I have dismissed the legal case of my servant or maidservant' — mišpāṭ (legal right/case/judgment); mĕʾēn (to refuse); ʿeḇed and ʾāmāh (male servant and female servant) — both gendered servants have legal standing in this oath. The willingness to acknowledge servant legal rights is remarkable; most ancient law systems gave the master almost total discretion over servants. bĕriḇām ʿimmāḏî — 'when they had a legal dispute with me' — rîḇ (dispute/lawsuit); ʿim (with/against); the servant as legal adversary of the master in a binding adjudication.
14: wĕmāh ʾeʿĕśeh kî-yāqûm ʾēl — 'what could I do when God rises up?' — qûm (to rise; used in legal contexts for the rising of a judge or accuser to pronounce judgment; cf. Isa 2:19,21 where God 'rises'); Job appeals to divine legal oversight as his ultimate accountability. wĕkî yip̄qōḏ māh ʾăšîḇennû — 'when he holds inquiry, what shall I answer him?' — pāqaḏ (to visit/examine/hold to account, the verb of divine visitation both in judgment and blessing; Exod 20:5; 32:34); the servant-equity clause flows from the recognition that God himself will audit Job's conduct toward those beneath him.
15: hălō-ḇabbeṭen ʿōśēnî ʿāśāhû — 'did not the one who made me in the womb make him also?' — the argument from common creatureliness: both master and servant were fashioned by the same divine Maker in the womb (bāṭen = womb/belly). wayyĕḵûnennû bāreḥem ʾeḥāḏ — 'and did not one God form us both in the womb?' — ʾeḥāḏ (one); rāḥem (womb); the theological anthropology here — one Creator, one womb, therefore one humanity that transcends status — anticipates Paul's 'there is neither slave nor free' (Gal 3:28) and James's appeal to a common creation as the ground for not discriminating between rich and poor (James 2:1-7).
16: ʾim-ʾemnaʿ mēḥēp̄eṣ dallîm — 'if I have withheld what the poor needed' — dallîm (the poor/weak, from ḏal = to hang thin/be weak; Exod 30:15; Lev 14:21); ḥēp̄eṣ (desire/what is wanted); withholding what the poor desired = failing to meet actual need. wĕʿênê ʾalmānāh ʾăkalleh — 'or caused the widow's eyes to fail from waiting' — kāllāh (to fail/be consumed; used in Ps 84:2 'my soul faints/kāllāh with longing'); the widow's eyes failing from sustained waiting — she has waited so long for help that even hope of it has been exhausted.
17: wĕʾōḵal pittî lĕḇaḏḏî — 'or if I ate my food alone' — pittāh (piece of bread/food; the most basic meal unit); lĕḇaḏḏ (alone/by oneself); the ethics of the shared table: in the ancient Near East, eating without sharing was a serious social offense. wĕlō-ʾāḵal yāṯôm mimmenāh — 'and the fatherless ate nothing from it' — yātôm (the fatherless child, whose special legal protection runs from Exod 22:22 through the Psalms and prophets); the shared meal as the concrete measure of social righteousness.
18: kî minnĕʿûrāy gĕḏēlānî kĕʾāḇ — 'for from my youth he grew up with me as with a father' — gāḏal (to grow up/be raised); the surprising identification of the fatherless as one who 'grew up with' Job as a father-figure. ûminneṭen ʾimmî ʾanḥennāh — 'and from birth I cared for the widow' — min-beṭen ʾem (from the womb of one's mother, a hyperbole for 'from earliest time'); nāḥāh (to guide/lead in the sense of pastoral care). Job's fathering of the fatherless and guiding of widows is presented as a lifelong character-formation, not occasional charity.
19: ʾim-ʾerʾeh ʾôḇēḏ mibbelî lāḇûš — 'if I have seen anyone perishing for lack of clothing' — ʾāḇaḏ (to perish/be lost; here with min-belî = 'without'); lāḇûš (clothing, the basic covering); the naked poor encountered (rāʾāh = seen) rather than helped. wĕʾên kĕsûṯ lāʾeḇyôn — 'or any poor person without covering' — ʾeḇyôn (the needy/destitute, a stronger economic term than dallîm); kĕsûṯ (covering/garment); the visual encounter with naked poverty as the trigger for the ethical obligation.
20: ʾim-lō bĕrăḵûnî ḥălāṣāyw — 'if he has not blessed me from his very loins' — ḥălāṣayim (the loins/hips, the body-part of warmth and strength; in Gen 35:11 'from your loins kings will come'); the blessing from the body = deep visceral gratitude, not polite thanks. ûmigēz kĕḇāśāy yiṯḥammām — 'and the wool of my sheep has not kept him warm' — gēz (shearing/fleece); the warm wool from Job's own flocks goes to warm the poor who bless him with embodied gratitude; practical material generosity confirmed by the recipient's bodily warmth.
21: ʾim-hănîp̄ôṯî ʿal-yāṯôm yāḏî — 'if I have raised my hand against the fatherless' — hānap̄ (to wave/brandish); the hand raised against the orphan as a gesture of threat or violence. kî ʾerʾeh bĕšaʿar ʿezrāṯî — 'when I see my support at the city gate' — šaʿar (city gate, the locus of judicial proceedings and public power in the ancient city; Gen 19:1; Ruth 4:1-2; Amos 5:12); ʿezrāh (support/help); to have allies at the gate = to have judicial power; Job swears he did not exploit that power against the most vulnerable.
22: kĕṯēp̄î missĕḵmāh tippôl — 'let my arm fall from its shoulder' — kāṯēp̄ (shoulder-blade/shoulder-joint); min-šĕḵem (from the shoulder); the imprecatory self-curse for using power against the fatherless: may the arm that could raise itself against the helpless be torn from its socket. wĕzĕrōʿî miqqāneh tiššāḇēr — 'and my arm be broken from the elbow' — zĕrôaʿ (arm/forearm, the seat of strength; used in Ezek 4:7; Deut 33:27 for the divine arm); tiššāḇēr = 'be broken/shattered'; the double anatomical curse (shoulder joint + forearm) ensures the arm of injustice is permanently disabled.
23: kî p̄aḥaḏ ʾēlî ʾêḏ ʾēl — 'for the destruction from God is a terror to me' — paḥaḏ (dread/terror; used in 3:25 for what Job feared coming upon him); ʾêḏ ʾēl = 'the calamity of God' (the divine disaster, also in Isa 10:3; Prov 1:26); Job's ethical behavior is motivated not only by compassion but by healthy fear of divine accountability. ûmiśśĕʾēṯô lō ʾûḵāl — 'before his majesty I cannot hold my ground' — śĕʾēṯô (his rising/majesty; the same word as 13:11); the towering divine majesty silences any thought of exploitation.
24: ʾim-śamtî zāhāḇ kislî — 'if I have made gold my trust/hope' — śwm + kislî (to set/place + my hope/security; kesel = the fat of the loins as seat of confidence, or simply confidence itself; cf. Ps 78:7 'to set their hope in God'); gold as alternative ground of security. wĕlakkettêm ʾāmartî mibṭaḥî — 'or called refined gold my security' — kéttem (fine gold, a poetic term appearing in 28:19; Ps 45:9; Prov 25:12; Song 5:11); miḇṭāḥ (trust/security; from bāṭaḥ = to trust; Ps 40:4; Jer 17:5-7 contrasts trust in humans with trust in God). The prohibition on financial security as primary trust is grounded here in a negative oath; Ps 52:7 and 1 Tim 6:17 make the same prohibition positive.
25: ʾim-ʾeśmaḥ kî-rāḇ ḥêlî — 'if I rejoiced that my wealth was great' — śāmaḥ (to rejoice/delight in; the delight-verb for Ps 97:12 'rejoice in the LORD'); rāḇ ḥayil = 'great wealth/army-force' (ḥayil = military force/wealth); joy in wealth-magnitude rather than in God. wĕkî-ḵaḇbîr māṣĕʾāh yāḏî — 'and that my hand had gained much' — kāḇîr (much/great; rare poetic word); the pride of self-made wealth as the spiritual danger. Job renounces both the accumulation-pride and the abundance-joy.
26: ʾim-ʾerʾeh ʾôr kî yāhēl — 'if I gazed at the light as it shines brilliantly' — ʾôr (light, the sun) yāhēl (shines brilliantly; from hālal in the sense of shining, the same root as hallelujah); wĕyārēaḥ yāqār hōlēḵ — 'and the moon as it moves in splendor' — yāqār (precious/glorious); astral worship through contemplative gazing. The prohibition on sun and moon worship runs from Deut 4:19; 17:3 through Jer 8:2; Ezek 8:16; the widespread nature of this temptation is reflected in how carefully Job includes it in his oath.
27: wayyippaṯ basseṯer libbî — 'and my heart was secretly seduced' — pāṯāh (to be enticed/seduced, the same verb as v9's heart-enticed-by-a-woman); the heart's private corruption — the inner liturgy of astral veneration that precedes any public act. wattišśaq-yāḏ lĕp̄î — 'and my hand was thrown a kiss toward them' — the kiss blown toward the celestial body (hand to mouth to the sky) was the classic gesture of astral worship known from Mesopotamian and Egyptian practice; 1 Kgs 19:18 uses the same idiom ('all who have not bowed to Baal and every mouth that has not kissed him').
28: gam-hûʾ ʿāwōn pĕlîlî — 'this too would be an iniquity deserving judgment' — the same legal formula as v11 (zimmāh + ʿāwōn pĕlîlî); sexual betrayal and idolatry are given identical legal weight. kî-ḵiḥaštî lāʾēl mimmāʿal — 'for I would have denied the God above' — kāḥaš (to deceive/deny by minimizing; used in Josh 7:11 for the denial of wrongdoing; Lev 19:11 'do not deny/deceive'); to worship the sun or moon is to deny ʾēl mimmāʿal (the God who is above/over) — the astral bodies are subordinate to the Creator, and worshiping them denies the hierarchy of being.
29: ʾim-ʾeśmaḥ bĕp̄îḏ śōnĕʾî — 'if I rejoiced at the ruin of one who hated me' — p̄îḏ (destruction/downfall; only here and 12:5; 21:20; 30:24; 31:3 — a rare Joban term for disaster); śōnēʾ (one who hates/is hostile); joy at an enemy's ruin. wĕhitʿôrartî kî-māṣāʾhû rāʿ — 'or exulted when disaster found him' — hitʿôrēr (to rouse/excite oneself in po'lel; to be stirred up with excitement); this oath clause anticipates Prov 24:17 ('do not rejoice when your enemy falls') and Christ's command to love enemies (Matt 5:44).
30: wĕlō-nāṯattî laḥăṭōʾ ḥiḵḵî — 'I did not let my mouth sin' — ḥēḵ (palate/mouth, the tasting organ of 12:11); nāṯan + laḥăṭōʾ = to give/allow to sin; the mouth as the instrument of verbal sin in asking God for the enemy's death. lišʾōl bĕʾālāh nap̄šô — 'by asking for his life with a curse' — ʾālāh (oath/curse; the solemn curse-word; Gen 24:41; Num 5:21); the prohibition on cursing the enemy to death extends the ethic of v29 from internal rejoicing to verbal action.
31: ʾim-lō ʾāmĕrû mĕṯê ʾāholî — 'if the men of my household have not said' — mĕṯê ʾōhel (men of the tent/household); the household testimony as evidence: what Job's own servants and family members say about his hospitality. mî-yittēn mibbĕśārô lō niśbāʿ — 'who has not been filled from his food?' — the rhetorical who-has-not?: everyone in the household has been fed to fullness (śāḇaʿ = to be satisfied/full) from Job's table.
32: ballayil lō-yālîn gēr — 'no stranger had to lodge in the street at night' — gēr (a stranger/sojourner, the protected alien resident whose care is commanded throughout the Mosaic law: Exod 22:21; 23:9; Lev 19:33-34; Deut 10:19); lûn (to spend the night, to lodge); the gate opened to the stranger at night is the hospitality ethic at its most demanding. ḏĕlatay lāʾōrēaḥ ʾep̄tāḥ — 'I opened my door to the traveller' — ʾōrēaḥ (the wayfarer/traveller; cf. Jer 14:8 where God is called 'like a stranger who turns aside to lodge'); the opened door to the traveler connects to the NT's 'do not neglect to show hospitality to strangers, for thereby some have entertained angels' (Heb 13:2).
33: ʾim-kissîṯî kĕʾāḏām pĕšāʿāy — 'if I have hidden my transgressions like Adam' — kāsāh (to hide/cover; the same verb as God's covering of nakedness in Gen 3:21); pešaʿ (transgression/rebellion); the explicit comparison to Adam's hiding his sin (Gen 3:8-12) is the only unambiguous reference in OT poetry to Adam's paradigmatic sin-concealment. This makes Job 31:33 the OT antecedent to Paul's Adam typology in Rom 5:12-14 and his treatment of covering and exposing transgression in 2 Cor 4:2. lĕṭamôn bĕḥûḇî ʿăwōnî — 'by concealing my iniquity in my heart' — ṭāman (to hide/bury; the verb of storing treasure or setting an ambush; Prov 2:1 stores wisdom; here iniquity is 'buried' in the chest/bosom).
34: kî ʾeʿĕrōṣ hāmôn rabbāh — 'because I feared the great crowd' — ʿāraṣ (to dread/be overwhelmed by; the root of marʿîṣ = terrifying; used for the numbing dread of the powerful); hāmôn rabbāh = the great crowd/multitude. ûbûz mišpāḥôṯ yĕḥiṯtannî — 'and the contempt of families/clans terrified me' — bûz (contempt/scorn; Prov 12:8; Ps 22:6 where the speaker is 'a worm and not a man... an object of scorn and contempt'); mišpāḥāh (clan/family group); the social pressure of communal shame is identified as the mechanism that keeps sin hidden 'like Adam': fear of public exposure rather than moral shame.
35: mî yittĕn-lî šōmēaʿ lî — 'O that someone would hear me!' — the repeated mî-yittēn optative ('O that!') of Job's greatest wish: a hearing. hēn-tāwî Šaddai yaʿănēnî — 'here is my signature — let Shaddai answer me!' — tāw is the last letter of the Hebrew alphabet (the equivalent of omega), written as an X-mark in ancient script; to place one's tāw = to sign one's legal document. Job formally signs and presents his petition to the divine court. wĕsēp̄er kāṯaḇ ʾîš rîḇî — 'O that my accuser had written out his charges!' — Job's longing for a written indictment so he could actually read and respond to the charges against him; the legal right to know one's accusation. This verse stands at the center of the book's legal metaphor: Job filing his signed petition against God's silence.
36: ʾim-lō ʿal-šikmî ʾeśśāʾennû — 'surely I would carry it on my shoulder' — šeḵem (shoulder, the body-part of burden-bearing; Isa 9:6 'the government shall be upon his shoulder'); Job would carry the divine indictment on his shoulder = bear it openly, proudly, as a sign of his willingness to be publicly examined. ʾeʿendehu kĕʿăṭārôṯ lî — 'and wear it as a crown on my head' — ʿăṭārāh (crown/garland; a mark of honor in Prov 12:4; 17:6; Isa 62:3); the indictment worn as a crown is the paradox of the wrongly-accused righteous person: what was meant to shame becomes the mark of honor.
37: mispār ṣĕʿāḏay ʾaggîḏennû — 'I would give him an account of all my steps' — mispār ṣĕʿāḏay (the number of my steps; the same forensic counting of 31:4 and 14:16); Job would render a complete account of every action. kĕnāgîḏ ʾeqrĕḇennû — 'I would approach him like a prince' — nāgîḏ (a leader/prince, one who goes in front; used of commanders: 1 Sam 9:16; 1 Chr 9:11); Job would not approach God as a cringing defendant but as a peer — standing upright, making a dignified legal presentation. The prince-analogy is the ethical climax of Job's self-defense.
38: ʾim-ʿālay ʾadmāṯî tizʿāq — 'if my land has cried out against me' — zāʿaq (to cry out; the same verb as the cry of Abel's blood in Gen 4:10 which 'cried out from the ground'); the land as a moral witness against its owner. wĕyaḥad tĕlāmêhā yiḇkāyûn — 'and its furrows have wept together' — telem (a furrow; the plough-line in Ps 65:10 that God's rain fills); weeping furrows personify agricultural land making a legal complaint. The oath extends from social ethics (servants, widows, orphans) to ecological ethics (land stewardship).
39: ʾim-ʾet-kōḥāh ʾāḵaltî ḇlî-ḵesep̄ — 'if I have eaten its produce without payment' — ḵōaḥ (power/produce; the productive force of the land); ḵesep̄ (silver/payment); consuming agricultural produce without paying its owners = land theft. wĕnep̄eš bĕʿālêhā hippāḥtî — 'or stripped the life/breath from its owners' — nāpah (to breathe/blow; pi'el hiṗpîaḥ = to blow out, to extinguish); nepeš bĕʿālāyw = the life of its owners; to consume the land's produce without payment is to extinguish the life-support of the legitimate owners. The ecological ethics connects directly to social ethics: land exploitation kills people.
40: taḥat ḥiṭṭāh yēṣēʾ ḥôaḥ wĕtaḥat-śĕʿōrāh bōʾšāh — 'let thistles grow instead of wheat and weeds instead of barley' — ḥôaḥ (thorn/thistle; the curse-vegetation of Gen 3:18 where 'thorns and thistles' mark the fall-curse on the ground); bōʾšāh (a noxious/stinking plant, perhaps the darnel); the reversal of cultivation-to-thorn-growth is the Gen 3 curse applied as a legal penalty. tammû diḇrê ʾîyôḇ — 'the words of Job are ended' — tāmam (to be complete/finished; the same root as Job's own tōm = integrity); the colophon formally closes Job's speeches. His last word before the divine answer is his signature of integrity.
